reason:
Both answers hit the key points solidly: cap liability at ~12 months fees (fallback 2x), narrow IP indemnity to unmodified-product infringement claims with standard remedy ladder, don't walk over negotiable boilerplate, and correctly frame the asymmetry (unlimited liability could bankrupt a two-person startup on a $40k deal) as the real issue. Both also suggest mutual consequential-damages waivers, cheap lawyer review, and E&O insurance as a lever.

Substantively they're a near-tie — B adds a couple of extra practical checks (insurance covenant compliance risk, auto-renewal/termination-for-convenience/audit rights) that A doesn't mention. But B is written in broken, telegraphic English throughout ("Enterprise expect redlines," "Silence look weak," "you not have," "This standard ask") which hurts readability and gives a less polished, less trustworthy feel for a decision a founder will act on. A is cleanly written, equally complete on the core asks, and easier to scan and act on quickly — better fit for a busy, smart asker despite being slightly less exhaustive on secondary items.
